title: DAVID — The Tongue the Conquest Wrote Down

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Classical Nahuatl — the language of the Aztec empire, and the rare case where the people who wrote it down were both the conquered and the conquerors.
Empire fell, pictographic books burned. Classical Nahuatl could have vanished with them — but it didn't go silent, and its descendants are spoken by well over a million people today.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?

The proof is the colonial archive: the Florentine Codex compiled with Nahua authors, grammars by friars like Andrés de Olmos, and thousands of legal documents Nahua scribes wrote in their own tongue using the Latin alphabet.

We rebuild Classical Nahuatl corpus-first: sixteenth-century grammars and dictionaries, the alphabetic spelling those scribes chose, and the living Nahuatl varieties that still carry the sound forward.

Because it was written alphabetically by people who spoke it, much of the pronunciation is well attested — though the saltillo glottal stop and vowel length were marked inconsistently, so some detail is reconstructed. I label each claim accordingly.
Listen — in xochitl in cuicatl — the flower, the song — a paired phrase meaning poetry itself, with the distinctive tl that ends so many Nahuatl words.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED TEXT · Classical Nahuatl · in xochitl in cuicatl

beats.speech_text: Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
